section.title: Contend for the Faith
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with a warm greeting to those who are called, loved, and kept, then pivots sharply: Jude meant to write about their shared salvation, but a threat forces a change of subject. Certain men have slipped in unnoticed, and the church must contend for the faith. These four verses set the whole letter in motion.
section.thesis: Because God keeps His own and has once for all delivered the faith, believers must earnestly contend for that faith against those who quietly pervert grace into license and deny the Lord who bought them.
section.frameNote: Two movements in one: a greeting rich with security, called, loved, kept; then the urgent occasion, a change of plan forced by intruders who twist grace and deny Christ.

verses.text: Jude, a {{doulos|servant}} of Jesus Christ and a brother of James, To those who are called, loved by God the Father, and {{tereo|kept}} in Jesus Christ:
Mercy, peace, and love be multiplied to you.

Beloved, although I made every effort to write to you about the salvation we share, I felt it necessary to write and urge you to {{epagonizomai|contend earnestly}} for the faith entrusted {{hapax|once for all}} to the saints.

For certain men have {{pareisduo|crept in among you unnoticed}}, {{asebes|ungodly}} ones who were designated long ago for condemnation. They turn the grace of our God into a license for {{aselgeia|immorality}}, and they deny our only {{despotes|Master}} and Lord, Jesus Christ.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant. Jude, likely a half brother of Jesus, does not lead with family privilege but calls himself a slave of Jesus Christ. It is a title of total belonging and glad submission to the Lord he once did not believe in, John 7:5.

tereo.gloss: To keep, guard, watch over, hold securely. This is a keyword that threads through the whole letter. Here believers are kept in Jesus Christ; later the ungodly are kept for judgment. The same God who guards His own also holds the wicked in reserve.

epagonizomai.gloss: To contend earnestly, to struggle or fight for. It is built on the word from which we get agonize, picturing an athlete straining or a soldier fighting. Jude urges not mild agreement but strenuous, costly defense of the faith against those who would corrupt it.

hapax.gloss: Once for all, once and never to be repeated. The faith was delivered once for all to the saints, a fixed and finished deposit of truth. It is not up for revision or reinvention; the church guards it and passes it on rather than adding to it.

pareisduo.gloss: To slip in secretly, to sneak in alongside, unnoticed. The danger did not storm the gates; it crept in quietly, from within the fellowship. The word captures the stealth of false teachers who look like insiders while undermining the faith.

asebes.gloss: Ungodly, impious, without reverence for God. This word and its relatives sound like a drumbeat through Jude, marking those whose lives and teaching show no true fear of God. Their condemnation was written long ago.

aselgeia.gloss: Sensuality, unrestrained indulgence, shameless excess. The intruders twist God's grace into a permit for it, treating forgiveness as freedom to sin. It names the moral rot that always follows when grace is severed from holiness.

despotes.gloss: Master, sovereign owner, the one with absolute rights over a household. To deny Jesus as our only Master is to reject His ownership and rule. Their license for sin is, at root, a refusal of the Lord who bought them.

basic.context: Jude, a brother of James and a half brother of Jesus, writes a short, urgent letter. He planned to write happily about the salvation Christians share, but news of trouble changed his plan. False teachers had quietly slipped into the churches, twisting God's grace into an excuse to sin and denying that Jesus is Lord. So Jude urges his readers to fight for the faith that God has handed down.
basic.hermeneutics: Jude wrote to a real church facing a real threat, and he expected them to act. The command to contend for the faith is not just ancient history; every generation has to guard the same gospel. Notice the balance: he first assures them they are called, loved, and kept, and only then calls them to fight. Security comes before the summons.
basic.originalLanguages: Jude calls himself a doulos, a slave of Jesus. Believers are tereo, kept, a word that runs right through the letter. The faith is delivered hapax, once for all, a settled deposit. The intruders pareisduo, crept in unnoticed, turning grace into aselgeia, sensuality, and denying our only despotes, Master.
basic.typology: Jude will soon reach back to Israel's history for warnings, but even here the pattern is old: the people of God always face those who creep in and corrupt from within, as false prophets did in Israel. The true and faithful Servant, Jesus, stands over against these unfaithful pretenders.
basic.christ: Jesus is named as our only Master and Lord, the one these teachers deny. He is also the one who keeps believers safe, we are kept in Jesus Christ. The whole letter is bracketed by His keeping power, from verse 1 to the closing promise that He is able to keep us from stumbling.
basic.god: God the Father loves His people and calls them to Himself. He is the God whose grace is holy, never a license to sin. And He is sovereign over judgment: the condemnation of the ungodly was designated long ago, so their success is never a threat to His plan.
basic.crossReferences: Second Peter 2 describes the same kind of false teachers who secretly bring in destructive heresies. Galatians 1 warns against any different gospel. And Jesus Himself warned of wolves in sheep's clothing who slip in among the flock.
basic.oneStory: From Eden onward, God's word has been twisted by those who promise freedom and deliver bondage. Jude calls the church to hold fast the faith once for all delivered, the gospel of the only Master, Jesus Christ, and to fight for it, secure in the knowledge that they are kept by the very Lord they contend for.

context.literary: The formal opening of a compact letter. Verses 1 to 2 are a standard greeting, but loaded with three great assurances, called, loved, kept. Verses 3 to 4 form the thesis statement of the whole book: the occasion, an urgent change of subject, and the reason, intruders described in a single damning sentence. Everything that follows unpacks verse 4.
context.historical: Jude, also called Judas, is most likely the brother of James the Just and thus a half brother of Jesus, Matthew 13:55. Writing probably in the AD 60s or later, he addresses churches infiltrated by traveling teachers who claimed the name of Christ while living immoral lives, a recurring peril in the first century.
context.canonical: Jude stands near the close of the New Testament as a fierce guardian of apostolic truth. It shares striking overlap with 2 Peter 2 and belongs to the general epistles' concern for the church's purity as the apostolic age drew toward its end.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To interrupt a planned letter of encouragement with an urgent call to defend the faith, alerting the churches to intruders who pervert grace and deny Christ, and to root that call in the security believers already have in God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The description is the danger, false teachers who have crept in. The prescription is enduring: contend earnestly for the faith once for all delivered. The command is not to be quarrelsome but to guard and stand for the gospel in every age.

debates.question: Who is the Jude who wrote this letter?

views.label: The brother of James and half brother of Jesus
views.case: He names himself brother of James, the well known leader of the Jerusalem church, and both are listed among Jesus' brothers in Matthew 13:55 and Mark 6:3. His humility in calling himself a servant rather than the Lord's brother fits a family member who came to faith after the resurrection.

views.label: Another Jude, such as the apostle Judas son of James
views.case: Some point to the apostle named in Luke 6:16, but that Jude is son of James, not brother, and the letter's link to James the Just is more natural.

debates.bearing: Either way the letter carries apostolic era authority. The strongest and traditional reading is that this is Jude the brother of James, giving the warning the weight of one who knew the Lord in the flesh yet bowed to Him as Master.

debates.question: What is the faith that was once for all delivered?

views.label: The objective body of gospel truth
views.case: The faith here is not personal trust but the content believed, the apostolic teaching about Christ. Delivered once for all, hapax, it is a fixed deposit to be guarded, not an evolving tradition.

views.label: A broader way of life and belief
views.case: Some read it more loosely as the Christian religion in general, but the sharp contrast with those who deny our only Master shows Jude means definite, doctrinal truth.

debates.bearing: Reading the faith as a settled deposit grounds the whole letter: there is something objective to contend for, and the false teachers can be measured against it and found wanting.

study.originalLanguages: The opening words set the tone. Jude is a doulos, a slave wholly owned by Jesus Christ. His readers are three things at once: called, loved by the Father, and tereo, kept, in Jesus Christ, a word that will recur to describe both the security of the saints and the reserved doom of the wicked. The faith was delivered hapax, once for all, a finished deposit. Against it stand men who pareisduo, slipped in unnoticed, who are asebes, ungodly, who turn grace into aselgeia, unbridled sensuality, and who deny our only despotes, Master. The single word epagonizomai, contend earnestly, sounds the letter's battle cry: strain and struggle, like an athlete or soldier, for the gospel.

typology.type: Faithful servants of God against intruders who corrupt from within
typology.fulfillment: A pattern running from the false prophets of Israel to the wolves Jesus warned of, fulfilled in the church's ongoing call to guard the deposit of truth in Christ.

typology.type: The only Master and Lord whom the ungodly deny
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, the true Sovereign and Owner of His people, whose lordship exposes every counterfeit gospel that severs grace from obedience.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center of these verses in two ways. He is the only Master and Lord, despotes and kyrios, whose absolute ownership the false teachers reject when they turn grace into license. And He is the keeper of His people: believers are kept in Jesus Christ, held fast by the very One they are called to contend for. The letter opens and closes with His keeping power, so the fight for the faith is never a fight in our own strength but under the protection of the Lord who bought us.
study.god: God the Father is the source of the believer's identity: they are called by Him, loved by Him, and kept for Christ. His grace is real and lavish, yet it is holy, and to twist it into a permit for sin is to attack His character. He is also the sovereign judge whose sentence on the ungodly was designated long ago, so no infiltration of the church catches Him by surprise or escapes His justice.

crossReferences.note: Parallel warning about false teachers who secretly bring in destructive heresies and deny the Master who bought them.
Paul pronounces a curse on anyone who preaches a gospel other than the one delivered, echoing Jude's concern to guard the faith.
Jesus warns of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves, the very stealth Jude describes.
Those who went out from us were not really of us, showing how threats to the faith often arise from within the fellowship.

study.oneStory: Since the garden, God's word has been twisted by voices promising freedom while delivering ruin. Jude joins the long line of those who guard the truth, calling the church to contend for the faith once for all delivered, the gospel of our only Master, Jesus Christ. And he grounds the fight in grace: those summoned to battle are already called, loved, and kept by the God whose story runs on to the day when Christ presents them faultless before His glory.

sermon.title: Reason for Writing
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Jude, this message treats Jude 1:3-4, the change of plan and the urgent call to contend for the faith once for all delivered, against those who pervert grace and deny Christ. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement's heart.

quiz.q: Why did Jude change what he had planned to write about?

options.text: A threat had arisen: ungodly men had crept into the church, so he urged them to contend for the faith.
options.note: Jude 1:3-4 names the intruders as the reason he wrote a call to arms instead.

options.text: He simply preferred to write about warnings rather than salvation.
options.note: It was necessity, not preference; he felt compelled by the danger.

options.text: He had run out of things to say about their shared salvation.
options.note: He longed to write about salvation but was forced to address the crisis.

options.text: The apostles told him to change his topic.
options.note: The text gives the intruders, not a command from the apostles, as the cause.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the faith was delivered once for all to the saints?

options.text: It is a settled, finished deposit of gospel truth to be guarded, not revised.
options.note: The word hapax means once for all; the faith is fixed and handed down.

options.text: It was delivered to only one generation and no longer applies.
options.note: Once for all means final and abiding, not limited to one generation.

options.text: It is a personal feeling of trust that each believer invents.
options.note: Here the faith is the objective body of truth believed, not merely private trust.

options.text: It can be updated and improved in every age.
options.note: The point is the opposite: it is delivered once for all and must be kept.

quiz.q: How does Jude describe the false teachers in verse 4?

options.text: Ungodly men who twist grace into license and deny Jesus as Master and Lord.
options.note: Jude 1:4 names both their immorality and their denial of Christ's lordship.

options.text: Sincere believers who simply made a few doctrinal mistakes.
options.note: Jude calls them ungodly and designated for condemnation, not mistaken believers.

options.text: Outsiders who openly attacked the church from a distance.
options.note: The danger was that they crept in unnoticed, from within, not from outside.

options.text: Faithful teachers whom Jude misunderstood.
options.note: The text presents them as genuinely ungodly, perverting grace and denying Christ.

quiz.q: What assurance does Jude give before he calls the church to fight?

options.text: They are called, loved by God the Father, and kept in Jesus Christ.
options.note: Jude 1:1 grounds the summons in the believer's security in God.

options.text: That they will never face any real danger.
options.note: He warns of real danger; the assurance is that God keeps them through it.

options.text: That the false teachers will quickly give up.
options.note: The letter expects a struggle, not an easy retreat by the intruders.

options.text: That their own effort will be enough to win.
options.note: The security rests in God's keeping, not merely in their own effort.

quiz.q: What lies at the root of turning grace into a license for immorality?

options.text: Denying Jesus as the only Master and Lord who owns and rules His people.
options.note: Jude 1:4 ties the moral rot to a rejection of Christ's lordship.

options.text: A minor disagreement about church customs.
options.note: It is far graver than custom; it is a denial of the Master Himself.

options.text: An honest confusion about what grace means.
options.note: It looks like confusion, but Jude names it as willful denial of the Lord.

options.text: Too much emphasis on holiness.
options.note: The problem is the abandonment of holiness, not an excess of it.

questions: Jude assures believers they are called, loved, and kept before he calls them to contend. How does resting in God's keeping change the way you face pressure on your faith?
To contend earnestly for the faith is strenuous, like an athlete or soldier. Where is God asking you to stand firm for the gospel rather than quietly letting it go?
The danger crept in unnoticed, from within the fellowship. How can a church stay watchful without becoming suspicious or unloving?
The false teachers turned grace into a license to sin. Are there ways you are tempted to treat God's grace as permission rather than as a call to holiness?
Jude calls Jesus our only Master and Lord. What would it look like this week to live more fully under His ownership of your whole life?

translationNotes.note: Kept in Jesus Christ can also be rendered kept for Jesus Christ; either way it stresses that believers are guarded by and held safe in Him.
The salvation we share is literally our common salvation; Jude had intended a warm letter on this theme before the crisis redirected him.
Where the BSB prints a dash after crept in among you unnoticed, it has been rendered as a comma per house style. License for immorality translates a word, aselgeia, meaning unrestrained sensuality.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jude 1:1-4. The dash in verse 4 has been rendered as a comma per house style.
